title: Building a rabbit hay rack
subtitle: A simple wall-mounted rack that keeps hay clean and reduces waste.
excerpt: A rabbit that eats hay off the cage floor eats some of it and sits in the rest. A hay rack mounted on the hutch wall holds hay at nose height, keeps it dry and clean, and halves the waste. Here's how to build a basic version from timber and wire mesh in a couple of hours.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit chapter. British Rabbit Council housing standards guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A holder for loose hay, typically mounted on a hutch wall, that keeps the hay accessible, dry, and off the floor.

aliases: making a hay rack for rabbits

content.text: Hay should make up the majority of a rabbit's diet, and a well-designed

makes that easier and cheaper, it keeps hay clean and accessible without the rabbit soiling it. The design below is a simple V-shaped rack made from two pieces of timber and a section of welded wire mesh, screwed to the inside wall of a hutch.
Two pieces of 18 mm × 100 mm timber, each 40 cm long, these form the back and base of the rack
A piece of 25 mm welded wire mesh, 40 cm × 30 cm, this forms the front face through which the rabbit pulls hay
A 40 cm length of 25 × 50 mm timber or a dowel for the front top edge, keeps the hay in
Screws, staple gun with 10 mm staples, and a drill

Screw the back board vertically to the hutch wall at rabbit nose height, around 10 cm from the floor for a small breed, 15,20 cm for a larger breed. The back board is the structural base everything else attaches to.

Screw the base board horizontally at the bottom of the back board, sticking out at 90° from the wall. This forms the floor of the hay compartment. Angle it very slightly down toward the wall to prevent hay falling forward.

Attach the wire mesh to the front face of the rack, stapled to the top of the base board and to the top of the back board. The mesh should bow slightly outward to create a V-shape, hay sits in the V and the rabbit pulls it through the mesh.
Add the front top bar or dowel across the top of the mesh to hold it in position.

A rack 40 cm wide holds enough hay for two or three rabbits for a day. Larger colony hutches need a rack per metre of wall space. Position it so the rabbit can pull hay out at face level without stretching up or crouching, a rack that's too high causes neck strain; one that's too low puts the rabbit's head in its own droppings.
